mission.title: Install and Configure Openkruise on Kubernetes
mission.description: OpenKruise is an incubating CNCF project that automates the management of large-scale applications on Kubernetes. It provides advanced workload controllers that enhance Kubernetes' native capabilities, making it easier to manage complex applications.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the OpenKruise Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add kubegems https://charts.kubegems.io/kubegems
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install OpenKruise using Helm
steps.description:
Install OpenKruise in the 'kruise-system' namespace, creating the namespace if it doesn't exist.
```bash
helm install openkruise kubegems/openkruise --namespace kruise-system --create-namespace --version 1.2.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the OpenKruise pods are running successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kruise-system
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
Configure resource limits for the OpenKruise deployment to ensure optimal performance.
```bash
kubectl set resources deployment openkruise --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --requests=cpu=250m,memory=256Mi -n kruise-system
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the OpenKruise Helm release from the 'kruise-system' namespace.
```bash
helm uninstall openkruise --namespace kruise-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs
uninstall.description:
Remove the Custom Resource Definitions associated with OpenKruise.
```bash
kubectl delete crd clonesets.apps.kruise.io
kubectl delete crd broadcastjobs.apps.kruise.io
kubectl delete crd sidecarsets.apps.kruise.io
kubectl delete crd statefulsets.apps.kruise.io
kubectl delete crd uniteddeployments.apps.kruise.io
kubectl delete crd daemonsets.apps.kruise.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'kruise-system' namespace to clean up all resources.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kruise-system
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Export the current configuration of OpenKruise for backup.
```bash
kubectl get all -n kruise-system -o yaml > kruise-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Ensure you have the latest version of the OpenKruise Helm chart.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade OpenKruise
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the OpenKruise installation to the latest version.
```bash
helm upgrade openkruise kubegems/openkruise --namespace kruise-system --version 1.2.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the OpenKruise pods to ensure they are running the new version.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kruise-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending
troubleshooting.description:
If OpenKruise pods are stuck in Pending state, check for insufficient resources or node availability.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n kruise-system
kubectl get nodes
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are in CrashLoopBackOff, check the logs for errors that may indicate misconfiguration.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kruise-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails, check for any pending migrations or resource conflicts.
```bash
helm history openkruise --namespace kruise-system
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of OpenKruise will show all pods in the 'kruise-system' namespace running with a status of 'Running'. You should also be able to manage advanced workloads using the OpenKruise controllers.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubegems https://charts.kubegems.io/kubegems
helm repo update
kubectl set resources deployment openkruise --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --requests=cpu=250m,memory=256Mi -n kruise-system

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have Helm and kubectl installed and configured to interact with your Kubernetes cluster.
